role_summary: Architect of the 1963 March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom; longtime nonviolent-direct-action strategist; A. Philip Randolph's deputy; openly gay civil rights organizer in an era hostile to that visibility.

photo.alt: Bayard Rustin at a news briefing on the Civil Rights March on Washington, Statler Hotel, August 27, 1963
photo.photographer: Warren K. Leffler, U.S. News & World Report Magazine
photo.repository: Wikimedia Commons (via Library of Congress Prints and Photographs Division)
photo.license: Public domain (Library of Congress: no known copyright restrictions; U.S. News & World Report collection donated to LoC)

ai_reading: The corpus discusses Rustin across seven transcripts: Phil Hutchings (entry 98, SNCC chairman 1968), the Ladner sisters joint interview (entry 75), Cleveland Sellers (entry 22), H. Jack Geiger (entry 54, Mississippi community-medicine), Doris Derby (entry 32), Lonnie C. King (entry 80, Atlanta sit-ins), and William S. Leventhal (entry 129). The cluster crosses SNCC, SCLC, and the Atlanta sit-in network, with the medical-organizing voice (Geiger) and the athlete-activist-network voice (Leventhal, close cosine to Russell and Carlos) as the surprising additions, suggesting Rustin's organizational vocabulary travels broadly across civil-rights cohorts the cultural reception treats as separate.
biographical_paragraph: Bayard Rustin is discussed in this corpus by 7 interviewees across the catalog (Phil Hutchings (entry 98), Ladners joint interview (Dorie Ann Ladner + Joyce Ann Ladner) (entry 75), Dr. Cleveland Sellers (entry 22), H. Jack Geiger (partial read) (entry 54), Dr. Doris Derby (entry 32), Lonnie C. King (entry 80), William S. Leventhal (entry 129)), but was not interviewed for the Civil Rights History Project; this catalog page treats Bayard Rustin as an external figure whose presence in the archive is mediated through other voices. The canonical scholarly source the catalog cites for biographical orientation appears as [src: 1] below.

interview_snippets.speaker: Phil Hutchings
interview_snippets.lead_in: Phil Hutchings, who chaired SNCC in 1968, recalls bringing Rustin to a debate event and places him in the New York organizing network the younger activists drew on:
interview_snippets.quote: And so we brought Bayard Rustin down, who was in some ways the godfather and mentor for a lot of these younger people from the New York crowd.

interview_snippets.speaker: Joseph Echols Lowery
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked whether he had contact with Rustin, SCLC co-founder Joseph Echols Lowery describes Rustin's repeated organizing work with the group in Montgomery (this transcript carries a publication-block audit tier; verify any quoted claim against external sources before publication):
interview_snippets.quote: But Bayard Rustin, yes he came down several times and worked with us for a long time.

interview_snippets.speaker: William S. Leventhal
interview_snippets.lead_in: William S. Leventhal lists the figures who led a movement orientation he attended, naming Rustin as its organizer and Norman Hill as Rustin's assistant:
interview_snippets.quote: Bayard Rustin organized A. Philip Randolph, Hosea Williams spoke to us and was at one of the organizers. Norman Hill, who had been at core, was Bayard's assistant.

interview_snippets.speaker: Lisa Anderson Todd
interview_snippets.lead_in: Lisa Anderson Todd identifies who ran a leadership meeting she attended, placing Rustin at its head rather than the labor and party figures present:
interview_snippets.quote: Bayard Rustin was conducting the meeting. Not Hubert Humphrey not Walter Reuther.

interview_snippets.speaker: Lawrence Guyot
interview_snippets.lead_in: Mississippi organizer Lawrence Guyot, discussing the period after the 1964 Democratic convention, points to Rustin's essay on shifting from protest to electoral politics:
interview_snippets.quote: Bayard Rustin comes out late after that with an article called From Protest to Politics

sources.title: John D'Emilio, Lost Prophet: The Life and Times of Bayard Rustin
